I'm guessing that this didn't turn out well for you. In the future, I want you to go right at it. Next time, you have to apologize for making a mistake. The fastest and most efficient means of establishing a quick working relationship is to acknowledge the negative and diffuse it. The last thing you want to do is make the person even angrier by attacking them or let their anger build inside by ignoring them. Anger is rarely, if ever, productive in you or the person you're negotiating with. Anger releases stress hormones and neurochemicals that can disrupt your ability to properly evaluate and respond to situations. When that happens, you basically get blinders on. You don't even realize that you're angry and you gain a false sense of confidence. This inhibits the productivity of the conversation because let's face it, no one wants to deal with someone who they think is going to punch them in the face. Next time you make a boneheaded mistake, I want you to address their anger right away. I've always found the phrase, look, I'm an asshole. To be a particularly effective way to make problems go away. The reason labeling works so well in deescalating angry confrontation is because it makes the other person acknowledge their feelings rather than continuing to act out. Here's an important point. Overlabeling isn't a problem. Sometimes we overlabel so much that the other side says, hey, hey, hey, don't be so hard on yourself. What just happened when they did that? They immediately stepped to our side. Labels don't plant negatives. Denials do. In addition to diffusing the situation, labeling is also a great way to turn a negative into a positive situation. If you diffuse the negative instead of ignoring it, the person feels like they're being heard and acknowledged and their mood will improve. In fact, research shows that the best way to deal with negativity is to observe it without reaction and without judgment. Then explicitly label the negative feelings to diffuse them. To truly be effective, great negotiators address the underlying emotions rather than ignoring them. A good rule of thumb to remember is labeling negatives diffuses while labeling positives reinforces. And remember, a negative has three times the emotional impact of an equal sized positive. So you'll get farther faster by being proactive with negatives.
